title: Daily Interview English
description: Daily 5-minute episodes: English patterns, interview frameworks, and speaking practice for tech professionals.
author: Daily Interview English

episodes.title: The Way I See It — 4-Second Pause Protocol
episodes.description: The very first episode. Today's pattern: 'The way I see it is...' for stating opinions without sounding defensive. Plus: the 4-Second Pause Protocol for unexpected interview questions.
episodes.pattern: The way I see it is...

episodes.title: What clicked for me was... — 4-Second Pause Protocol
episodes.description: Welcome to Daily Interview English. Episode 2, July 05, 2026.  Today's episode draws from Changelog: "From open source hits to OpenAI (Interview)".  Today's pattern: "What clicked for me was..."  Use this to explain a key realization or motivation. It's more natural than 'I realized' and signals genuine insight.  Here are three examples. Repeat each one out loud after me.  Example 1. What clicked for me was realizing that coding agents let me build software tailored to a team's exact workflow.

episodes.title: The way I see it is... — STAR-LA Framework
episodes.description: Welcome to Daily Interview English. Episode 3, July 05, 2026.  Today's episode draws from Changelog: "From open source hits to OpenAI (Interview)".  Today's pattern: "The way I see it is..."  States an opinion without sounding rigid. Signals 'this is my view but I'm open to discussion.'  Here are three examples. Repeat each one out loud after me.  Example 1. The way I see it is, we should optimize for latency first and add caching layers later.  Example 2. The way I see it is, this trade-off fav
episodes.pattern: The way I see it is...

episodes.title: That said... — Three Ways to Say I Don't Know
episodes.description: Welcome to Daily Interview English. Episode 4, July 05, 2026.  Today's pattern: "That said..."  Introduces a counterpoint or qualification. More natural than 'however' in speech.  Here are three examples. Repeat each one out loud after me.  Example 1. That said, the trade-off was worth it given the timeline we were working with.  Example 2. That said, I'd do the migration differently if I were starting today.  Example 3. That said, the approach worked well for our scale and I'd recommend it for
episodes.tip: Three Ways to Say I Don't Know

episodes.title: What I'd really highlight is... — 4-Second Pause Protocol
episodes.description: Welcome to Daily Interview English. Episode 5, July 05, 2026.  Today's pattern: "What I'd really highlight is..."  Directs attention to the most important part. Shows you know what matters and what's detail.  Here are three examples. Repeat each one out loud after me.  Example 1. What I'd really highlight is that the adoption was organic — nobody mandated these tools.  Example 2. What I'd really highlight is how the team's velocity changed after we rebuilt the workflow.  Example 3. What I'd real

episodes.title: To put a finer point on it... — 4-Second Pause Protocol
episodes.description: Welcome to Daily Interview English. Episode 6, July 05, 2026.  Today's pattern: "To put a finer point on it..."  Refines a previous statement to be more precise or impactful. Shows you're thinking critically about your own words.  Here are three examples. Repeat each one out loud after me.  Example 1. To put a finer point on it — we didn't just speed up triage. We eliminated the need for manual triage entirely.  Example 2. To put a finer point on it — the real innovation wasn't the AI. It was re
episodes.pattern: To put a finer point on it...

episodes.title: I haven't worked directly with that. What I can tell you is... — 4-Second Pause Protocol
episodes.description: Welcome to Daily Interview English. Episode 7, July 05, 2026.  Today's pattern: "I haven't worked directly with that. What I can tell you is..."  Honest about gaps, confident about adjacent experience. Transforms a weakness into a demonstration of learning ability.  Here are three examples. Repeat each one out loud after me.  Example 1. I haven't worked directly with Kubernetes at that scale. What I can tell you is that I've designed distributed systems handling 50K requests per second, and the
episodes.pattern: I haven't worked directly with that. What I can tell you is...

episodes.title: This resulted in... — 4-Second Pause Protocol
episodes.description: Welcome to Daily Interview English. Episode 8, July 05, 2026.  Today's pattern: "This resulted in..."  Simple, direct cause-to-effect. Always follow with a number or specific outcome.  Here are three examples. Repeat each one out loud after me.  Example 1. This resulted in the CVE workload dropping by 80 percent within the first month.  Example 2. This resulted in the BA team adopting the agents without any formal rollout — they just started using them.  Example 3. This resulted in us shipping t

Four English patterns you can use in real engineering conversations. Two interview frameworks to structure your answers. A discussion of today's article. episodes.title: Self-Introduction Practice — Present-Past-Future
episodes.description: Special practice episode: your self-introduction using the Present-Past-Future framework. Covers trading systems experience, legacy migration, early coding agent adoption, vulnerability platform, security tool ($100K+ subscription replacement), internal dev platform, outside interests, and the interview close. Includes section-by-section breakdown, memory anchors, and a 60-second backup.

episodes.title: Airwallex Prepared Q&A — 7 Key Interview Questions
episodes.description: Special practice episode: prepared answers for 7 key interview questions. Tell me about yourself, Why this company, Why leaving current role, Built something from zero, Worked with non-engineers, Security approach, What you're looking for. Each uses STAR-LA or Structure-First with your real stories. Includes key numbers quick reference.

episodes.title: System Design: Scalability Fundamentals
episodes.description: ============================================================ System Design Playlist — Auto-generated Episode Date: 2026-07-08 Concepts: Scalability Fundamentals, CAP Theorem, SQL Fundamentals ============================================================  [INTRO — 30 sec]  Welcome to the System Design Playlist. You're in phase 1 with 0 concepts mastered out of 25. Today we're reviewing 3 concepts that are due for spaced repetition.   ============================================================ SEG

episodes.title: System Design Interviews — The Complete Framework
episodes.description: Sriram Panyam (ex-Google, ex-LinkedIn) breaks down the system design interview: 5-phase approach, 25 canonical problems, what interviewers look for at each level, and the key mindset shift from buzzword-driven to requirement-driven design. The single best SDI resource.

episodes.title: Event Sourcing & CQRS — Architecture Deep Dive
episodes.description: Martin Dilger (author of Understanding Event Sourcing) explains event stores, projections, CQRS, event versioning, and why Kafka is NOT an event store. Practical adoption strategy: 2-day modeling workshop + 1-day build.

episodes.title: Rate Limiting & High-Traffic Systems — Real Incident Walkthrough
episodes.description: Mojtaba Sarooghi (Queue-it) on handling flash traffic: 100K to 100M requests in one minute, cold-start failures, DynamoDB at 200K TPS, rate limiting algorithms, and bot detection (98% of traffic is bots during ticket drops).

episodes.title: Message Queues & Event-Driven Architecture — Patterns & Tradeoffs
episodes.description: Chris Patterson (MassTransit creator) on message brokers vs event streaming, sagas vs routing slips, transactional outbox pattern, dead-letter queues. Transport-agnostic architectural patterns for distributed systems.

episodes.title: Consistent Hashing Explained (ByteByteGo)
episodes.description: Visual explainer on consistent hashing — how distributed systems assign keys to nodes and minimize redistribution when nodes are added or removed. Core concept for sharding and distributed caches.

episodes.title: CAP Theorem Simplified (ByteByteGo)
episodes.description: Why distributed systems must choose between Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance. Clear explanation with real-world database examples (CP vs AP systems).

episodes.title: Rate Limiter Design — Token Bucket & Leaky Bucket (ByteByteGo)
episodes.description: Token bucket, leaky bucket, fixed window, sliding window — the four rate limiting algorithms explained. How to scale rate limiters in a distributed system.

episodes.title: How a URL Shortener Works — System Design Walkthrough (ByteByteGo)
episodes.description: End-to-end system design for a URL shortener: hash functions, base62 encoding, database schema, caching strategy, and scaling to millions of URLs.

episodes.title: API Gateway Explained (ByteByteGo)
episodes.description: What an API gateway does: authentication, rate limiting, request routing, protocol translation, and service discovery. How it fits into a microservices architecture.

episodes.title: Mock Interview: Cross-Border Payments
episodes.description: Full mock system design interview: designing a multi-currency payment pipeline with FX conversion, idempotency, compliance screening, and bank connectivity. Two-voice dialogue between interviewer and candidate.

episodes.title: Mock Interview: Distributed Cache
episodes.description: Full mock system design interview: designing a Redis-like distributed caching system with consistent hashing, virtual nodes, replication, and rebalancing. Two-voice dialogue.

episodes.title: Mock Interview: Notification System
episodes.description: Full mock system design interview: designing a multi-channel notification system supporting push, email, and SMS with templates, preferences, and delivery tracking. Two-voice dialogue.

episodes.title: Mock Interview: Typeahead / Search Autocomplete
episodes.description: Full mock system design interview: designing a sub-50ms autocomplete system using tries, pre-computed suggestions, and Lambda architecture for freshness. Two-voice dialogue.

episodes.title: Mock Interview: Object Storage (S3)
episodes.description: Full mock system design interview: designing a durable object storage system with erasure coding, multipart upload, and 11-nines durability. Two-voice dialogue.

episodes.title: Mock Interview: FX & Multi-Currency Ledger
episodes.description: Full mock system design interview: designing an FX rate engine with rate locking and a per-currency double-entry ledger — directly relevant to Airwallex. Two-voice dialogue.

episodes.title: Mock Interview: Top-K / Heavy Hitters
episodes.description: Full mock system design interview: finding the most frequent items in a data stream at scale using Count-Min Sketch, heaps, and sliding window algorithms. Two-voice dialogue.

episodes.title: Mock Interview: Real-Time Chat
episodes.description: Full mock system design interview: designing a WhatsApp-like messaging system with WebSocket delivery, read receipts, and group chat fan-out. Two-voice dialogue.

episodes.title: Mock Interview: Location-Based Service
episodes.description: Full mock system design interview: designing nearby-driver search and real-time location tracking using geohash, quadtree, and S2 geometry. Two-voice dialogue.

system-design.title: System Design — Spaced Repetition Learning
system-design.description: Curated audio materials for system design interview prep. SE Radio deep dives + ByteByteGo visual explainers. Mapped to SM-2 spaced repetition concepts in the generic-tutor.

sd-mock-interviews.title: Mock System Design Interviews
sd-mock-interviews.description: Full-length mock interviews with two-voice dialogue — interviewer asks, candidate thinks aloud, catches mistakes, and receives feedback.
